sections.text: Welcome back to AP Biology. This is Module Three — Membrane Transport. We're inside College Board Units Two and Three, looking at the plasma membrane and how anything gets across it. This topic is the bridge from cell structure into everything that comes next — signaling, energetics, the nervous system. Let's get into it.

Right now, in every nerve cell in your body, sodium-potassium pumps are moving three sodium ions out and two potassium ions in. Each pump cycles roughly a hundred times every second, and your nerve cells carry millions of them. Together those pumps burn around twenty percent of your resting energy budget. If it stopped for even a few minutes, you would lose consciousness. That pump is what today is about.

Here is the plan. First — the membrane itself, the fluid mosaic, and what can and can't cross it. Second — passive transport, where stuff slides down its gradient for free, including the all-important osmosis. Third — active transport, pumps and cotransport and bulk endocytosis, where the cell spends ATP to move things uphill.

The plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer — two sheets of phospholipids with hydrophilic phosphate heads facing the watery outside and inside, and hydrophobic fatty acid tails tucked in the middle. Embedded in that bilayer are proteins: integral proteins that span all the way through and act as channels, carriers, pumps, and receptors; and peripheral proteins that just sit on one face. On the outside, carbohydrate chains attached to proteins and lipids form glycoproteins and glycolipids that handle cell recognition. Cholesterol sits in there too, keeping the membrane fluid at body temperature. This is the fluid mosaic model — Singer and Nicolson, nineteen seventy-two.

The membrane is selectively permeable — meaning some things cross freely, others need help, and some can't cross at all without active work. The rule is simple. Small nonpolar molecules — oxygen, carbon dioxide, steroid hormones — slip right through the hydrophobic core. Small polar uncharged molecules like water and urea cross slowly. But large polar molecules like glucose, and anything with a charge — sodium, potassium, calcium, chloride — those need a transport protein. The bilayer's hydrophobic interior is the gatekeeper.

Passive transport moves things down the concentration gradient — high to low — and it costs zero ATP. Two flavors. Simple diffusion is the small nonpolar molecules crossing the bilayer directly. Facilitated diffusion uses an integral protein but still no ATP. Channel proteins are hydrophilic tunnels — like aquaporins for water, or ion channels for sodium and potassium. Carrier proteins bind the molecule, change shape, and release it on the other side — glucose transporter GLUT-1 is the classic example. Both are passive. Both go downhill. The protein is just the door.

Osmosis is a special case — the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane. Water moves from where there's MORE water — meaning less solute — toward where there's LESS water — meaning more solute. Tonicity describes the solution around the cell. Hypotonic means the outside has less solute than the cell, so water rushes IN; an animal cell swells and lyses, but a plant cell becomes turgid because the cell wall stops it from bursting. Hypertonic means the outside has more solute, so water leaves; animal cells shrivel — crenation — and plant cells plasmolyze. Isotonic — no net movement. Remember: hypertonic means high SOLUTE, low water. Never the reverse.

Now active transport — moving things AGAINST the gradient, low to high, which requires energy. Primary active transport hydrolyzes ATP directly. The headline example is the sodium-potassium pump — the Na/K ATPase. For each ATP it spends, it pumps three sodium ions OUT of the cell and two potassium ions IN. That builds an electrochemical gradient — more sodium outside, more potassium inside, and the inside slightly negative — which the cell then uses for nerve impulses, muscle contraction, and as fuel for the next slide.

Secondary active transport is the clever one. The cell doesn't burn fresh ATP — it uses the gradient that primary transport already built. In the small intestine, a sodium-glucose symporter lets sodium flow back into the cell down its steep gradient, and that downhill flow drags glucose along with it, UP its own gradient. Symporters carry both molecules the same direction; antiporters carry them opposite directions. Either way, the energy comes from the sodium gradient the Na/K pump made — so it's still ATP, just one step removed.

Try this one. You drop a human red blood cell into pure distilled water. Then you drop a plant cell — say, an onion epidermis cell — into the same distilled water. What happens to each? Be specific about the direction of water movement, what happens to the cell volume, and whether either cell bursts. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Answers. Distilled water is hypotonic to both cells — almost no solute outside, lots of solute inside — so in both cases water rushes INTO the cell. The red blood cell, with only a plasma membrane, swells until it bursts. That's called lysis or hemolysis. The plant cell also takes in water and swells, but the rigid cellulose cell wall stops it from bursting. Instead, it becomes turgid — pressed firm against the wall — which is actually the healthy state for a plant cell. Same hypotonic environment, totally different outcome, because of the cell wall.

For really big stuff — whole bacteria, large proteins, droplets of fluid — the cell uses bulk transport. Endocytosis is when the membrane wraps around material and pinches off a vesicle going IN. Three types: phagocytosis is cellular eating — your white blood cells engulfing bacteria. Pinocytosis is cellular drinking — taking in extracellular fluid. Receptor-mediated endocytosis is selective — specific ligands bind specific receptors first, then get pulled in together. Exocytosis is the reverse — a vesicle fuses with the plasma membrane and dumps its contents OUT. That's how neurons release neurotransmitters and how your pancreas secretes insulin. All of this uses ATP.

Where does this matter in your body? Kidneys — your nephrons use sodium-potassium pumps and aquaporins to reabsorb water and ions and decide what to pee out. Neurons — every action potential is the sodium and potassium gradients you spent ATP to build, collapsing in a controlled wave down the axon. Intestinal cells — sodium-glucose cotransport is literally how the sugar from your breakfast gets from the gut lumen into your blood. Membrane transport isn't an abstract topic. It's how you think, how you absorb food, and how you stay hydrated.

Two confusions trip up almost every AP Bio student. On the left — the common wrong intuitions. On the right — what's actually true. Read these carefully. The wording of an exam question will live or die on these distinctions.

Recap. The membrane is a fluid mosaic — phospholipid bilayer plus embedded proteins. Selective permeability: small nonpolar stuff free, charged or large polar needs a protein. Passive transport runs downhill, no ATP — simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion through channels or carriers, and osmosis for water. Active transport runs uphill and burns ATP — the Na/K pump moves three out, two in, per ATP. Secondary active transport rides on the gradient primary built. And bulk transport uses vesicles — endocytosis in, exocytosis out.

Your path forward. Read OpenStax Biology Chapter Five — that's the deep version of everything we just covered. Drill the Khan Academy AP Biology problem sets on membrane transport and tonicity — the tonicity questions especially repay practice. Do the assignment in your dashboard. And book an advisor check-in if osmosis direction or active versus passive still feel slippery. Next time, we move into Module Four — Cell Communication, where these same membrane proteins start carrying signals.
